segments.purgatorio-21-g47: "Quei" is an archaic demonstrative/pronoun meaning 'that one' used here as 'he' (the speaker who speaks next); "cominciò" is passato remoto, 3rd singular of cominciare ('he began'), and the colon marks the start of direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-21-g48: The phrase "Cosa non è che ..." is a rhetorical/negative-existential construction meaning 'Is there anything that ...?'; "Cosa" = 'anything/something', "non è" = 'is not/does not exist', and "che" introduces the relative clause. "sanza" is the archaic form of "senza" ('without') and "ordine" means 'order/arrangement'. "senta" is present subjunctive (3rd singular of sentire) used in the relative clause after the negative construction, here meaning 'feels/experiences' or 'is affected by'. "la religïone de la montagna" literally 'the mountain's religiosity/devotion' (religious spirit associated with the mountain).
segments.purgatorio-21-g49: "o" = 'or' introducing an alternative; "che" again introduces the subordinate clause; "sia" is present subjunctive (3rd singular of essere) used to express potentiality/possibility ('that it be'); "fuor" is an archaic form of "fuori" ('outside'); "d'" is the contracted form of "di"; and "usanza" means 'custom' — together "fuor d'usanza" = 'outside custom / against customary practice'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g50: "Libero è qui" places the predicate adjective before the verb by inversion for emphasis: 'Here it is free'. "da ogne alterazione" uses the archaic "ogne" (= "ogni") and the preposition "da" to mean 'from every alteration/change', i.e. 'free from any corruption or change'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g51: This is a relative/prepositional clause with a following infinitival clause: "di quel che 'l ciel da sé in sé riceve" = 'of that which Heaven receives from itself, in itself' ("da sé in sé" is emphatic reflexive phrasing). The sequence "esser ci puote" is inverted: the infinitive "esser" ('to be') comes before the clitic "ci" ('to/for us') and the archaic verb form "puote" (poetic/old form corresponding to modern "può"/"può essere") meaning 'can'. The whole means 'of that which Heaven receives in itself there can be for us being' → 'that which Heaven receives can be to us a cause of being'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g52: "e non d’altro" is a short coordinating phrase: "e" = 'and', "non" negates, and "d'altro" (contraction of "di altro") means 'of anything else' — it closes the contrast begun in the previous clause, i.e. 'and not from any other source'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g53: The single word "cagione" ('cause/reason') is the noun that completes the preceding clause: syntactically it is the complement governed by the infinitive phrase earlier ("esser ci puote cagione") — 'can be a cause for us'. Placing it at the end is a standard poetic order for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-21-g54: Per che here functions idiomatically as 'therefore' or 'wherefore' introducing the result clause; che is the relative/connector 'that'; non is the negation; pioggia is a noun 'rain'. The three words together begin a catalogue of things that do not fall higher up.
segments.purgatorio-21-g55: non negates the following item. grando in Danteic usage is an archaic/noun form for 'hail' (modern Italian grandine); although the supplied lemma/ senses list treats grare, here the word is best read as the noun 'hail'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g56: non + neve: simple negation + noun 'snow'. The short phrase continues the list of precipitations that do not reach higher up.
segments.purgatorio-21-g57: non + rugiada: again negation plus the noun 'dew' (rugiada). The items are coordinated and elliptical (no verb repeated until 'cade' later).
segments.purgatorio-21-g58: This is the completion of the negated list with the finite verb: non (negation) + brina (here the noun 'hoarfrost') + più sù 'any higher up' (più = 'more/any further', sù = 'up'); cade is 3rd person singular present indicative 'falls'. che introduces the comparative clause 'than' (here 'than the short ladder of three steps'); la scaletta di tre gradi breve = 'the short little ladder of three steps' (breve modifies scaletta).
segments.purgatorio-21-g59: nuvole spesse = 'thick/dense clouds' (spesse here = 'thick/dense', not 'often'); non paion is elliptical for non paiono — paion is an archaic 3rd-person plural present of parere 'appear' (paion = 'seem/appear'); né ... né introduces further negatives; rade = 'sparse'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g60: né coruscar continues the negative series: né = 'nor'; coruscar (in context) denotes lightning/brief flashing — the infinitive/verb form is used nominally to mean 'flash/lightning' or 'a flash'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g61: né figlia di Taumante: 'nor the daughter of Thaumas' — figlia = 'daughter' (here a descendant/product); di = 'of'; Taumante is the proper name Thaumas (mythological), so 'daughter of Thaumas' (i.e., Iris).
segments.purgatorio-21-g62: che introduces a relative clause modifying 'figlia di Taumante': di là = 'over there' or 'on that side'; cangia is present indicative 'changes'; sovente = 'often'; contrade = 'districts/quarters' (here 'regions/quarters' where she varies).
segments.purgatorio-21-g63: secco vapor = 'dry vapor/mist' (secco = 'dry'); non surge più avante = 'does not rise any farther forward' (surge = archaic/present infinitive or subjunctive form of surgire but here used as 3rd-person singular present indicative 'rises' in the poem's diction); più = 'any more/further'; avante = 'forward/beyond'; ch’ al sommo d’i tre gradi ch’io parlai = 'than to the summit of the three steps I spoke of' (ch’ = che 'that/than'; al sommo = 'to the top of'; d’i = 'of the'; ch’io parlai = relative clause 'which I mentioned'—parlai is past tense 'I spoke').
segments.purgatorio-21-g64: dov’ = dove 'where'; ha = 3rd-person singular present of avere 'has' (here 'has' = 'holds'/'has placed'); ’l vicario di Pietro = 'the vicar of Peter' (the Pope's representative); le piante = 'the soles/feet' (piante = 'soles' here, figuratively 'where he plants his feet'). The clause locates the summit as the place where the papal vicar stands.
segments.purgatorio-21-g65: Trema = 3rd-person singular present 'it/there trembles'; forse = 'perhaps'; più giù = 'further down' (più = 'more/any further', giù = 'down'); poco o assai = 'a little or a lot' (poco = 'little', o = 'or', assai = 'much').
segments.purgatorio-21-g66: ma = 'but'; per vento = 'because of/through wind' (per expresses cause here); che ’n terra si nasconda = a relative/subordinate clause 'which hides itself in the earth' — nasconda is present subjunctive (subordinate clause describing a characteristic of the wind), si = reflexive 'itself'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g67: non so come = 'I do not know how' (non = negation, so = 1st-person singular present of sapere 'I know', come = 'how'); this parenthetical fixes the speaker's uncertainty about the cause.
segments.purgatorio-21-g68: qua sù = 'here up' (qua = 'here', sù = 'up'); non tremò mai = 'it never trembled' — tremò is historical/poetic perfect (passato remoto) 3rd-person singular 'trembled', mai = 'ever/never' (with negation = 'never').
segments.purgatorio-21-g160: Ed is the archaic/poetic form of e (“and”) and simply links this line to the previous action. Ei is the subject pronoun “he” (placed after the conjunction for rhythm). Surgendo is a present participle (from surgere/surgendere), here used adverbially—"rising"—to indicate the speaker’s motion as he begins to speak; the colon marks direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-21-g161: Or = now. Puoi is the second-person singular present of potere ("you can") used with the infinitive comprender (modern comprendere) which follows and means "to grasp/understand"; the object la quantitate (the quantity/measure) sits between the modal verb and the infinitive. De l’amor = "of the love" (de + the article); ch’ is the contracted relative che ("that") introducing the relative clause. A te mi scalda literally places the directional phrase a te ("toward you") before the clitic mi ("me") and the verb scalda ("warms/ inflames"), so the clause means "that warms me toward you."
segments.purgatorio-21-g162: Quand’ is the elided form of quando ("when"). Io is the emphatic subject pronoun "I." Dismento is first-person present (here "I forget" or "I set aside" in the sense of putting aside our concern); nostra vanitate is the direct object phrase "our vanity/futility." The comma pauses before the participial clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-21-g163: Trattando is a present participle/gerund (from trattare) meaning "treating" or "dealing with," and it indicates the manner or condition concurrent with the forgetting just mentioned. L’ombre = le ombre (the shades/shadows); come = "as" or "as if"; cosa is "thing/matter" and is the noun modified by salda, an adjective meaning "solid, firm, substantial." Grammatically, "trattando l’ombre come cosa salda" means "treating the shades as a solid thing" (i.e., regarding shadowy souls as if they were substantial).
segments.purgatorio-21-g123: Volser is a past (poetic/archaic) form of volgere meaning 'turned' and governs the following direct object phrase; word order is inverted (verb before subject) as a common Dantean poetic device. Virgilio is the subject (proper name), and a me queste parole is the prepositional/accusative phrase 'toward me these words' (a me = 'to me'). Con viso che introduces a relative clause modifying viso ('with a look which...').
segments.purgatorio-21-g124: Tacendo is a present gerund of tacere ('being silent / while silent') and here gives the manner of the look: the face 'being silent' (i.e. silent in speech) yet communicating. The gerund links directly into the relative clause begun by che in the previous segment.
segments.purgatorio-21-g125: Disse is the 3rd-person singular past of dire ('said') and is the main verb of the relative clause ('which ... said'). 'Taci' is the quoted imperative (2nd person singular) of tacere: a direct command, 'Be silent.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g126: Ma introduces a contrast ('but'). Non può tutto la virtù che vuole is a clause with può (present indicative of potere, 'is able / can') and tutto modifying the sense 'everything' — literally, 'virtue cannot [do] everything that it wants.' La virtù here functions as 'virtue' or 'power/authority' rather than a strictly moral quality; che vuole is a relative clause 'that it wants.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g127: Ché introduces the reason ('for / because'). Riso e pianto son tanto seguaci a la passion di che ciascun si spicca is a causal clause: 'laughter and tears are such followers/attendants of the passion by which each one is marked/stands out.' Son is the poetic contraction for sono ('are'), seguaci is a plural noun 'followers/attendants,' and si spicca is reflexive 'is distinguished/stands out' (i.e. the passion that distinguishes each person).
segments.purgatorio-21-g128: This relative clause (che) completes the thought: 'that they follow the will less in the most truthful.' Men 'less' modifies the verb seguon (3rd-person plural present of seguire, 'they follow'). Voler is here a substantivized infinitive meaning 'the will' rather than an active verb. Ne’ is the contracted preposition ('in the') and più veraci is a superlative phrase 'the most truthful' used substantively (i.e. 'the truest people'). The word order (men seguon voler ne’ più veraci) places the negation/adverb men before the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-21-g129: Io pur sorrisi places subject + adverb before the past tense verb: Io (I) + pur (even/also) + sorrisi (passato remoto 'I smiled'). The simile come l’uom ch’ammicca uses come + noun + relative clause (ch’ = che, who/that) with ammicca in the present indicative meaning “who winks.”
segments.purgatorio-21-g130: per che is a causal/resultive phrase (’whereupon/for which reason’). l’ombra si tacque uses the reflexive si + tacque (passato remoto of tacere) to mean 'the shade/ghost fell silent.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g131: e riguardommi is riguardò + enclitic mi written in verse form → 'and he regarded/looked at me.' ne li occhi is the prepositional phrase 'in the eyes.' ove ’l sembiante più si ficca is a relative clause: ove (where) + 'the countenance' (sembiante) + si ficca (present reflexive of ficcare, 'fixes itself/settles'), i.e. 'where his expression is most fixed.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g132: e introduces the speech. Se here begins the quotation and should be read as 'if' (conditional), but the provided sense list did not include that meaning; tanto labore in bene assommi combines tanto (so much) + labore (toil) + in bene assommi (assommi = 3rd sg. present/subjunctive/indicative of assommare, 'amounts to' / 'adds up to' + in bene = 'to good').
segments.purgatorio-21-g133: disse is the passato remoto 3rd person of dire: 'he said,' marking the reporting verb for the subsequent quoted question.
segments.purgatorio-21-g134: The quoted question: perché = 'why'; la tua faccia testeso uses testeso (emphatic reflexive/adverb) to stress 'your very face itself'; un lampeggiar di riso is a nominal phrase 'a flash/gleam of laughter'; dimostrommi is mostrare + enclitic mi (3rd person form + me) meaning 'shows me' or 'do you show me?' in the interrogative sense.
segments.purgatorio-21-g135: Sentence-final punctuation (period) marking the end of the preceding line/utterance.
segments.purgatorio-21-g136: Or (now) + son (sono, 'I am') + io: inverted word order common in verse. d’una parte e d’altra preso uses preso (past participle) adjectivally: 'taken/seized on one side and on the other.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g137: l’una mi fa tacer: l’una 'the one' + fa + infinitive tacer = 'makes/causes me to be silent' (fare + infinitive causative construction).
segments.purgatorio-21-g138: l’altra scongiura is 'the other implores/entreats' (scongiura here is a verb). ch’io dica uses the subordinating che + first-person present subjunctive dica ('that I should say/speak'), introducing what the other asks him to do.
segments.purgatorio-21-g139: ond’ (onde/ond) is an archaic connective 'whereupon/therefore,' followed by io sospiro 'I sigh' (present tense here narratively describing his reaction).
segments.purgatorio-21-g140: e sono inteso: sono + past participle inteso = 'I am understood/heard/attended to'; dal mio maestro is an agent phrase 'by my master' (da + il → dal). The participle inteso here conveys being listened to or attended to by the master.
segments.purgatorio-21-g141: Quoted imperative: Non aver paura uses the infinitive aver with a prohibitive non to give the command 'do not be afraid' (infinitive used idiomatically in commands).
segments.purgatorio-21-g142: mi dice is the present 'he says to me' (mi = indirect object 'to me', dice = 3rd sg. of dire), introducing further quoted instruction.
segments.purgatorio-21-g143: di parlar: di + infinitive used to mark the object/purpose 'to speak' (i.e. 'do not be afraid to speak'). The infinitive follows the prohibition in the quote.
segments.purgatorio-21-g144: ma parla e digli is two imperatives: parla ('speak') and digli ('tell him' = di + gli fused into digli). quel ch’e’ dimanda is a fused relative clause: quel 'that' + ch’ (che = 'that') + e’ (here representing 'egli', 'he') dimanda ('asks') → 'what he asks.' con cotanta cura = 'with such care' (cotanta intensifies cura).
segments.purgatorio-21-g20: Come here is an exclamatory interjection expressing surprise or incredulity. In older Italian poetry a single-word exclamation like this stands alone and is rendered in English as “How!” or “What!” depending on register.
segments.purgatorio-21-g21: This segment is a comma (punctuation) that continues the reporting of speech; no lexical items to analyse.
segments.purgatorio-21-g22: diss' is an elided form of disse (said); the apostrophe shows the omitted vowel. elli is an older third-person pronoun used here in direct speech to mark the speaker ('he/that one said'), placed after the verb in the poetic line.
segments.purgatorio-21-g23: e = and, a simple coordinating conjunction. parte here functions adverbially or idiomatically (literally 'in part' / 'aside'), andavam is the imperfect first-person plural of andare ('we were going' / 'we went on'); forte is an adverb meaning 'strongly' or 'quickly' in context so the clause means 'and we went on briskly.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g24: se is the conditional conjunction 'if'. voi is the second-person plural pronoun 'you'. siete is the present indicative (you are). ombre is the plural noun 'shades' or 'ghosts' as the predicate noun. che introduces a relative clause modifying ombre; Dio sù = 'God on high' (sù = 'up/above', archaism). non degni here means 'does not deign/esteem' — an older/elliptical usage where degni functions as a verb-like predicate ('does not deign'), so the relative clause says 'whom God on high does not deign (to regard)'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g25: chi is the interrogative 'who'. v' is the contracted form of vi (to/for you, here 'you'); ha is third-person singular of avere used as auxiliary 'has'. per la sua scala = 'up/along his ladder/stair' (per = through/along/by means of). tanto scorte: scorte is a past participle (from scortare = 'to escort/lead'), agreeing with the addressed persons; the whole clause asks 'who has escorted you so far along his ladder?'
segments.purgatorio-21-g26: A period marking the end of the sentence and of the quoted question; no lexical items to analyse.
segments.purgatorio-21-g27: E = 'and', here introducing the speaker's reply. 'l' is the elided definite article (il) before a consonant in poetry; dottor = 'doctor' or 'teacher' (Virgil as Dante's teacher); mio is the postposed possessive 'my' (a common poetic order: 'the doctor mine' = 'my teacher'). The colon marks the start of direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-21-g28: Se introduces a conditional 'if'. tu is the explicit subject 'you'. riguardi is present (second-person singular) of riguardare meaning 'you look at/consider'. a' is the contracted preposition a (here 'at/to') followed by segni 'signs' (direct object). che introduces a relative clause; questi ('this one' or 'he') is the subject of the relative, and porta is present 'bears/wears/carries' (i.e. 'the signs he bears'). e che l'angel profila adds a second relative: 'and that the angel profila (outlines/sets forth)'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g29: ben vedrai is future indicative (vedrai = 'you will see') with ben ('well' / 'clearly' / 'indeed') = 'you will clearly see'. che introduces the subordinate clause. coi buon is a contraction of con i buoni ('with the good/with the righteous'). convien is an impersonal present (conviene) meaning 'it is fitting/necessary'. ch'e' regni is 'that he reign' — ch' = che, e' = egli (he) contracted in poetry, and regni is the present subjunctive ('should reign' or 'may reign'), so the clause means 'that he should reign with the good.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g69: Tremaci is the verb tremare with the enclitic ci indicating location ('here/there'); it functions as a 3rd-person present (they/it tremble) with ci attached. Quando introduces a temporal clause ('whenever'); alcuna is an indefinite adjective 'some/any' modifying anima; monda is an adjective meaning 'clean, purified'; sentesi is a reflexive form (si attached) of sentire, here meaning 'feels itself/hears itself' in the sense 'feels'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g70: Sì che introduces a result/so-that clause ('so that'); surga and si mova are present subjunctive 3rd-person singular forms (subjunctive used for potential/result). The reflexive si marks that the soul moves herself; per + infinitive (per salir) expresses purpose ('in order to climb up'); sù is the adverb 'up'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g71: E is the coordinating 'and'; tal is the demonstrative/adjective 'such'; grido is a noun 'cry/call'; seconda here is a verb (3rd-person singular present of secondare) meaning 'follows' or 'comes after', so the phrase says that such a cry follows (answers/ensues).
segments.purgatorio-21-g72: De la mondizia is a prepositional phrase 'of the purification/purity' (de + article + noun). Sol is the archaic form of 'solo' meaning 'only' and modifies voler used here as a noun (the infinitive voler functions substantively as 'the will'); fa is 3rd-person singular 'makes/does' and prova is a noun 'test/proof' — together 'the will alone makes the test of purification'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g73: Che is a relative/explanatory conjunction here introducing the following clause that explains what is meant by the previous statement ('for/which').
segments.purgatorio-21-g74: Tutto is adverbial here ('completely/entirely') modifying libero ('free'). A + infinitive (a mutar) expresses capacity/permission or direction toward the action 'to change'; convento (literally 'convent') is used as 'dwelling/condition' — 'entirely free to change its dwelling.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g75: L’alma is the direct object 'the soul' (l' = the article + alma). Sorprende is 3rd-person singular present of sorprendere here meaning 'seizes/overtakes' (not merely 'surprises') and takes l'alma as its object: 'it seizes the soul.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g76: E 'and'; di + infinitive (di voler) links the action 'to will' as the matter in question ('in willing' / 'as to the will'); le is the indirect object pronoun 'to her' (referring back to the soul); giova is 3rd-person singular present of giovare meaning 'is useful / benefits' — 'and it benefits her in willing.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g77: Prima is an adverb 'first/at first'; vuol is 3rd-person singular present of volere ('wants/intends'); ben (bene) is an adverb 'well/good', so the short clause means 'at first (it) desires well' or 'initially the will is good.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g78: Ma 'but'; non 'not'; lascia is 3rd-person singular present of lasciare ('leaves, abandons'); il talento is the direct object 'the inclination/desire' (talento here = inclination). Che introduces the relative clause that follows, with divina giustizia as the subject of that clause. The word order places the main verb before the relative clause.
segments.purgatorio-21-g79: Contra is a preposition 'against' (archaic form of contro) and voglia is 'will/desire'; together 'contra voglia' means 'against her will' or 'contrary to the will.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g80: Come 'as' or 'in the way that'; fu is the 3rd-person singular past of essere ('was'); al peccar uses the preposition a + the infinitive peccar ('to sin' used substantively) to mean 'in sinning' or 'at the time of sinning' — 'as it was when sinning.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g81: Pone is 3rd-person singular present of porre ('puts/places'); al is the contraction a + il ('to the'); tormento is the noun 'torment/punishment' — 'places (her) in torment.' The verb belongs to the relative clause introduced by che, whose subject is 'divina giustizia.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g82: E io = simple coordinate 'And I' (E = 'and', io = 'I'), starting the speaker's personal statement.
segments.purgatorio-21-g83: che son giaciuto a questa doglia cinquecent’ anni e più = 'who have lain in this pain five hundred years and more': che = relative 'who'; son = 1st-person singular present 'I am' used here as auxiliary in a poetic perfect sense; giaciuto = past participle 'lain' (to lie/lie dead/lie in this suffering); a questa doglia = 'in this sorrow/pain'; cinquecent’ anni e più = 'five hundred years and more' (elliptical orthography cinquecent' = cinquecento).
segments.purgatorio-21-g84: pur mo sentii libera volontà di miglior soglia: = 'even now I felt a free will toward a better threshold': pur = 'even/still'; mo = 'now/just now'; sentii = 1st-singular past 'I heard/felt' (here 'felt' inner movement); libera volontà = 'free will/resolve'; di miglior soglia = 'toward a better threshold' (miglior = contracted form of migliore, soglia = 'threshold').
segments.purgatorio-21-g85: però sentisti il tremoto e li pii spiriti per lo monte render lode a quel Segnor, = 'therefore you heard the tremor and the pious spirits through the mountain render praise to that Lord': però = 'therefore'; sentisti = 2nd-person singular 'you heard/felt'; il tremoto = 'the tremor/earthquake'; li pii spiriti = archaic plural 'the pious spirits' (li = plural article); per lo monte = 'throughout the mountain' (per = 'through'); render lode = 'to render/give praise' (render = 'give/render' + lode 'praise'); a quel Segnor = 'to that Lord' (Segnor = 'Lord', here meaning God).
segments.purgatorio-21-g86: che tosto sù li ’nvii = relative clause qualifying 'that Lord': che = 'who'; tosto = 'soon/straightaway/immediately'; sù = 'up'; li = plural article 'them'; ’nvii is Dantean/abbreviated form of 'invia'/'inviò'/'inviare' used here as present-tense 'sends' (he sends them up).
segments.purgatorio-21-g1: La sete natural = 'the natural thirst' (noun phrase). che mai non sazia = relative clause with che referring back to sete; mai non sazia is an imperfect/general statement 'never satiates' (mai + non = 'never'). se non con l’acqua = 'except with the water' where se non = 'except' (literally 'if not') and con = 'with/by means of'. onde = relative/adverbial 'wherewith/with which' introducing the following clause (onde la femminetta samaritana domandò la grazia = 'with which the little Samaritan woman asked for grace'). domandò is passato remoto 'asked/asked for' and la grazia is the direct object.
segments.purgatorio-21-g2: mi travagliava = verb phrase where travagliava is the imperfect of travagliar(e) 'to torment/afflict' and mi is the clitic object 'me': 'it tormented me' (imperfect, ongoing or habitual past action). The subject of this verb is the preceding phrase 'La sete natural...'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g3: e pungeami la fretta = 'and haste pricked me.' pungeami is an archaic/clitic form (pungea + mi → pungeami) meaning 'was stinging/pricking me' (imperfect tense; the clitic mi attaches to the verb). per la ’mpacciata via = 'because of/on account of the awkward/embarrassed way' where per here has a causal value and ’mpacciata is impacciata with the initial i elided after the article; dietro al mio duca = 'behind my guide/leader' (dietro + a + il → al).
segments.purgatorio-21-g4: e condoleami a la giusta vendetta = 'and I felt compassion/sympathy for the just vengeance.' condoleami is an older/clitic verb form (condolea + mi → condoleami) expressing 'I felt sympathy/was grieved'; a la indicates the target 'toward the just vengeance.' The verb is first-person (I) in sense (past/continuing in context).
segments.purgatorio-21-g5: Ed ecco = conjunction + interjection; Ed is simply e ('and') with euphonic d, and ecco is the demonstrative/interjection 'behold' or 'here'. The comma marks a pause before the explanatory clause that follows.
segments.purgatorio-21-g6: sì come ne scrive Luca = 'just as Luke records of it.' sì come = 'just as'; ne is a prorhetical/partitive pronoun 'of it/about it' referring back to what was just said; scrive is present tense 'writes/records' (common in literary reference to Scripture); Luca is the evangelist (proper name). che Cristo apparve a’ due ch’erano in via = relative clause 'that Christ appeared to the two who were on the way.' apparve is passato remoto 'appeared'; a’ due = a + i → ai (contracted) 'to the two'; ch’erano = che erano 'who were.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g7: già surto fuor de la sepulcral buca = 'already risen out of the sepulchral pit.' già = 'already'; surto is a past participial/participial-like form meaning 'risen' (archaic/poetic); fuor de = 'out of'; sepulcral buca = 'sepulchral pit/tomb.' The phrase modifies the preceding clause about Christ appearing.
segments.purgatorio-21-g8: ci apparve un’ombra = 'a shade appeared to us.' ci is the indirect-object/clitic 'to us'; apparve is passato remoto 'appeared'; un’ombra = 'a shadow/a shade.' Word order places the indirect object before the verb (common in older Italian).
segments.purgatorio-21-g9: e dietro a noi venìa = 'and was coming behind us.' venìa is the imperfect 'was coming' (progressive sense); dietro a noi = 'behind us.' The imperfect continues the narrative action concomitant with the appearance.
segments.purgatorio-21-g10: dal piè guardando la turba che giace = 'looking from the foot at the crowd that lies (there).' dal = da + il 'from the'; piè = 'foot' or 'base' (here 'the foot of the slope'); guardando is a present participle 'looking' or 'while looking'; la turba che giace = 'the crowd that lies (prostrate/there).' The participle describes the manner of the approaching shade.
segments.purgatorio-21-g11: né ci addemmo di lei = 'nor did we take notice of her.' né = 'nor/not even'; ci addemmo is first-person plural passato remoto (addere/addemmo) used idiomatically here to mean 'we paid attention/attended'; di lei = 'of her.' The negative particle negates the whole clause.
segments.purgatorio-21-g12: sì parlò pria = 'for she spoke first.' parlò is passato remoto 'spoke/said'; pria = 'before/first'; sì here has a confirmatory/temporal sense 'thus/then' linking to her speaking before they noticed. The phrase explains why they had not attended to her.
segments.purgatorio-21-g13: dicendo: = present participle 'saying:' introducing direct speech. It links the narrative clause to the quotation that follows.
segments.purgatorio-21-g14: «O frati miei, = vocative/exclamation 'O my brothers,' addressing those present; O is the interjection 'O/Oh,' frati is the plural of frate 'brothers' (here spiritual/brethren), miei is the possessive 'my.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g15: Dio vi dea pace». = 'May God grant you peace.' Dio = 'God' (subject). vi is the clitic/dative 'to you' (plural). dea is the present subjunctive of dare used as an optative 'may (he) give' (third-person singular subjunctive). pace = 'peace.' The clause is a blessing or wish in the subjunctive mood.
segments.purgatorio-21-g16: Noi ci volgemmo sùbiti = 'We turned ourselves immediately.' Noi is subject 'we'; ci is the reflexive clitic; volgemmo is passato remoto (volgere) 'we turned/turned about'; sùbiti is adverb 'at once/immediately.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g17: e Virgilio rendéli ’l cenno ch’a ciò si conface = 'and Virgil made/returned the sign that befits this.' rendéli is an older form with an enclitic (render + li) 'gave/returned to them/it' or 'made (for them)'; l (l’) cenno = 'the sign' (cenno = nod/gesture); ch’a ciò si conface = relative clause 'which suits this' (ch’ = che; a ciò = 'to/for that/this'; si conface from confacere 'to be fitting,' present tense 'is fitting'). The verb + clitic ordering and the relative clause are typical of literary/older Italian.
segments.purgatorio-21-g18: Poi cominciò: = 'Then he began:' Poi = 'then/afterwards'; cominciò is passato remoto 'began.' The colon introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-21-g19: «Nel beato concilio ti ponga in pace la verace corte che me rilega ne l’etterno essilio». = 'May the true court that consigns me to eternal exile place you in peace in the blessed council.' Nel beato concilio = 'in the blessed council' (nel = in + il). ti ponga in pace = ponga is the present subjunctive of porre used optatively 'may (it) place/put you in peace'; ti is the clitic object 'you.' la verace corte is the grammatical subject 'the true court' placed after the verb (inversion for stylistic effect). che me rilega ne l’etterno essilio = relative clause 'which relegates/me consigns me into eternal exile' (rilega present tense = 'relegates').
segments.purgatorio-21-g104: Tanto (an adverb of degree) appears before the copula fu (passato remoto of essere) to give a resultive force: “so/so much was”. The noun phrase mio vocale spirto places the possessive mio before the adjective and noun (my vocal spirit), with vocale modifying spirto; the whole phrase functions as the subject of fu.
segments.purgatorio-21-g105: Che introduces the result clause (‘that…’) linking the sweetness of his voice to what follows; here it functions as a conjunction (not an interrogative).
segments.purgatorio-21-g106: Tolosano is an epithet set off by commas (appositive). It is an adjectival/demonymic label (literally “Tolosan” / “of Toulouse”) inserted as a parenthetic qualification; its exact referent (whether it labels Roma or the spirit) is determined by the context, but syntactically it is an apposition.
segments.purgatorio-21-g107: This is an inverted clause: a sé (to herself) is a reflexive prepositional phrase, mi is the clitic direct object ‘me’ placed before the verb trasse (passato remoto of trarre, ‘drew/pulled’), and Roma is the subject appearing after the verb for poetic order. Literally: “Rome drew me to herself.”
segments.purgatorio-21-g108: Dove introduces a relative/adverbial clause of place (‘where’). Mertai is an archaic past (first person) meaning ‘I merited/was worthy’ (merti‑/meritai), followed by the direct object le tempie (‘the temples’). Ornar di mirto is an infinitival phrase ‘to adorn with myrtle’ where di marks the material/element (mirto = myrtle).
segments.purgatorio-21-g109: Syntactically the clause reads with predicate first: the name Stazio is what follows from the verb noma. La gente (the people) is the subject and ancor di là (‘still over there’) qualifies their naming; mi is the clitic object ‘me’ placed before the verb noma (present tense ‘call/name’).
segments.purgatorio-21-g110: Cantai is passato remoto first person ‘I sang’ (perfective past) and takes di Tebe as its object/complement: ‘I sang (about) Thebes.’ The order is straightforward epic diction.
segments.purgatorio-21-g111: E poi (‘and then’) links the next theme; del (di + il) grande Achille is a prepositional phrase ‘of the great Achilles’ indicating the object/topic of the later song.
segments.purgatorio-21-g112: Ma (but) introduces the contrast. Caddi is passato remoto ‘I fell’; in via = ‘on the road’ or ‘along the way’; con la seconda soma is a prepositional phrase ‘with the second load/burden’ (soma used metaphorically). The verb is first-person narrative past.
segments.purgatorio-21-g113: Al mio ardor (‘to/for my ardour’) opens the clause; the verb is placed after this phrase in a poetic order. Fuor is an archaic/poetic past-form (here behaving like an old plural past ‘furon’ = ‘they were’) and seme is a predicate noun ‘seed’ so that le faville (the sparks) is the subject: literally, ‘The sparks were seed to my ardour.’
segments.purgatorio-21-g114: Che introduces a relative clause modifying faville: che mi scaldar = ‘which warmed me.’ Scaldar appears in a truncated/poetic form (final vowel dropped) functioning like a past or resultive verb meaning ‘warmed’ (or an infinitive used relatively); mi is the clitic object.
segments.purgatorio-21-g115: De la divina fiamma is a simple prepositional phrase ‘from the divine flame.’ Onde is a relative/adverbial pronoun meaning ‘from which/whence’ (not the common literal senses ‘wave’); sono allumati is a present passive construction ‘are kindled/have been lit,’ with allumati as past participle, and più di mille quantifies the number.
segments.purgatorio-21-g116: De l’Eneïda dico = ‘I speak of / I mean the Aeneid.’ Dico is present-tense ‘I say’ used to identify the referent just named (the ‘divine flame’).
segments.purgatorio-21-g117: La qual (‘which’) is a relative pronoun referring to the Aeneid; mamma is the predicate ‘mother’ and fummi is an archaic/poetic form meaning ‘was to me’ (essentially ‘fu a me’), so the clause reads ‘which was a mother to me.’ The order is inverted for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-21-g118: E fummi nutrice continues the same structure: e = ‘and’, fummi (same archaic verbal form) = ‘was to me’, and nutrice = ‘nurse/nourisher’ as predicate; together, ‘and was my nurse.’
segments.purgatorio-21-g119: Poetando is a gerund/participial form meaning ‘in composing’ or ‘while composing poetry,’ giving the circumstance in which the Aeneid was mother and nurse.
segments.purgatorio-21-g120: Sanz’ (contraction of senza) = ‘without’; essa refers back to the Aeneid; non fermai is negative + passato remoto ‘I did not stop / I would not have stopped’ (here elliptical/conditional in sense), and peso di dramma = ‘the burden/weight of drama’ (peso as ‘burden’ with di dramma marking its kind).
segments.purgatorio-21-g121: E per esser vivuto di là quando visse Virgilio = 'And because he had lived over there when Virgil lived': per esser vivuto = causal 'because of having lived' (esser vivuto = perfect infinitive/participial sense 'having lived'); di là = 'over there'; quando visse Virgilio = temporal clause 'when Virgil lived'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g122: assentirei un sole più che non deggio al mio uscir di bando = 'I would assent (consent) one sun more than I ought to for my coming out of banishment': assentirei = conditional 'I would assent/approve'; un sole = literal 'one sun' (here a metonymy for 'one more day' or 'one more period of daylight'); più che non deggio = 'more than I ought' (che ... non used comparatively = 'than'); al mio uscir di bando = 'to my going out from the ban' (uscir = verbal noun 'going out/exit', di bando = 'from banishment/exile').
segments.purgatorio-21-g30: This is a causal clause introduced by Ma perché ("But because"). Lei is the subject ('she'); che introduces a relative clause describing her: dì e notte fila is a present-tense relative clause meaning 'she spins day and night' (fila = filare, 3rd person singular present). Non li avea tratta ancora la conocchia is an older word-order: avere + past participle (avea + tratta) = pluperfect/had drawn; li is the preverbal clitic 'them'; ancora = 'yet/still'; la conocchia is the noun phrase (the distaff) that is the thing that has not been drawn. The final che + clause (che Cloto impone a ciascuno e compila) is another relative clause modifying la conocchia; impone is present tense used as a general/truth-of-fact present.
segments.purgatorio-21-g31: L’anima sua = 'his/its soul' (article + noun + possessive). The elided form l’ marks the definite article before a vowel; sua is the possessive agreeing with anima. This is a nominative phrase naming the soul in question.
segments.purgatorio-21-g32: Ch’è = che + è, a relative/identifying clause 'which is' or 'who is.' Tua e mia serocchia = literally 'your and my serocchia' (serocchia is an archaic word for 'sister'); the two possessives together make the sense 'our sister.' The verb è is present tense linking the noun phrase to the previous reference.
segments.purgatorio-21-g33: Venendo sù is a gerund + adverbial particle meaning 'coming up' or 'on coming up'; it gives the circumstance/manner of the action that follows. Venendo is the gerund of venire, and sù = 'up'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g34: Non potea venir sola is an imperfect-tense statement: potea (imperfect of potere) = 'could' / 'was able'; non...sola = 'could not come alone.' Venir is the infinitive governed by potea; sola is an adjective 'alone.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g35: Però ch’ = 'because/for that' introducing the reason. Al nostro modo = 'in our manner' (al = a + il, nostro modo = our way). Non adocchia uses an archaic verb adocchiare meaning 'to fit, to suit, to make appropriate'; here it means she does not fit (our) mode, giving the reason she could not come alone.
segments.purgatorio-21-g36: Ond’ is a literary/poetic 'whence' (from which); io fui tratto is a perfect/passive idea formed with fui + past participle tratto = 'I was drawn' (fui tratto = I was drawn out); fuor = 'out'; de l’ampia gola d’inferno = 'from the wide throat of Hell' (de = from/of). Per mostrarli is purpose: mostrare + enclitic li = 'to show them'; the enclitic attaches to the infinitive.
segments.purgatorio-21-g37: E is 'and.' Mosterrolli here is a poetic/archaic form with an enclitic -li (to them) attached; functionally it repeats the idea of mostrarli ('and to show them further') — mosterrolli is an archaism or dialectal/inflexional form of mostrar + enclitic. Oltre = 'beyond/further.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g38: Quanto introduces an extent clause 'how much / as far as'; ’l is the clitic/article (here standing for the object previously referenced, i.e. 'them' or 'it'); potrà is future tense of potere = 'will be able'; menar is the infinitive 'to lead' or 'to bring'; mia scola = 'my school/teaching' (scola = teaching). The construction means 'as far as my teaching will be able to lead (them).'
segments.purgatorio-21-g39: Ma = 'but'; dimmi is the imperative 'tell me' (di- + mi enclitic). This is a direct address/request from the speaker to the addressee.
segments.purgatorio-21-g40: Se tu sai is a conditional interrogative 'if you know' (se = 'if'; tu = 'you'; sai = present of sapere 'know'). It softens the question by asking whether the addressee has knowledge.
segments.purgatorio-21-g41: Perché introduces 'why.' Tai crolli = 'such crashes/rumblings' (tai = such; crolli = plural noun from crollo, 'collapse/crash'); diè dianzi 'gave just now' is an idiomatic use where diè (gave) takes the noun crolli as thing given — 'the mountain gave such crashes' — and l' monte is the subject delayed to the end, a common poetic inversion.
segments.purgatorio-21-g42: E perché = 'and why'; tutto ad una = 'all at once' (tutto = everything/all; ad una = at one [time]); parve = passato remoto 'seemed'; gridare = infinitive 'to cry out' (used with parve); infino a’ suoi piè molli = 'even down to its soft/weak feet' (infino a = up to/even to; suoi piè = its feet/base; molli = 'soft/weak'). The clause describes the extent of the apparent crying out.
segments.purgatorio-21-g43: Sì mi diè = 'Thus/so he gave me' or 'so he answered me.' Sì = 'so/thus'; mi = indirect object 'to me'; diè = perfect/past 'gave' (passato remoto of dare) used here as 'he gave (me that reply)'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g44: Dimandando is a present participle/gerund 'asking' (dimandare = to ask/request); it describes the manner in which he gave the reply ('he gave me [the answer], asking').
segments.purgatorio-21-g45: Per la cruna del mio disio is a figurative circumstantial phrase: per = 'through' or 'by'; la cruna = 'the eye (of a needle)/narrow opening'; del mio disio = 'of my desire.' Together: 'through the (needle-)eye of my desire' — a poetic image describing how the question was posed or perceived.
segments.purgatorio-21-g46: Che introduces the content of what was given/said ('that'). Pur con la speranza = 'even/still with hope' (pur = still/even; con la speranza = with hope). Si fece la mia sete = reflexive si + fece (past of fare) here means 'was made/became' — 'my thirst was made/was rendered' — and men digiuna = 'less fasting' i.e. 'less famished' or 'less like fasting.' The phrase means 'that even with hope my thirst was made less hungry/famished.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g87: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' or 'so'. Ne is the clitic pronoun 'of/about it' referring back to the matter just mentioned. Disse is the passato remoto (third person singular) of dire: 'he said'. Together: 'Thus he said of it.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g88: This subordinate clause begins with e però ch’ (here però = causal 'because/therefore' + ch’ = che 'that'). El is the subject pronoun 'he' (older/dialectal form). Si gode is reflexive present 'enjoys himself' (godere), and tanto ... quant’ ... è grande la sete is a comparative/degree structure: 'so much in drinking as (in proportion to) how great the thirst is'. Del = di + il; ber is the archaic poetic infinitive 'bere' 'to drink'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g89: Non saprei dir is a modal expression with saprei (conditional 'I would know / I could') plus the infinitive dir(e): 'I couldn't say' or 'I would not know how to say'. Quant' here = quanto 'how' (in what degree). El mi fece prode uses fece (passato remoto of fare) 'made' and prode (adjective) 'valiant/brave', so 'how brave he made me.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g90: E is the conjunction 'and'. L' is the elided definite article; savio (adjective) before duca (noun) yields 'the wise duke' — here duca = guide (Virgil), not necessarily a noble duke.
segments.purgatorio-21-g91: Omai 'by now' + veggio (archaic veggiare) used in the sense 'I perceive/watch' the thing named. La rete 'the net/snare' is the direct object; che introduces a relative clause. Qui vi 'mpiglia: qui = here, vi = you (object clitic, 'you there'), 'impiglia' (colloquial/poetic impiglia) = 'entangles/catches'. E come si scalappia: 'and how it scalappia' — si is reflexive/impersonal and scalappia (scalappiare) describes a noisy struggling or stamping movement (how it thrashes/struggles).
segments.purgatorio-21-g92: Perché here introduces a reason/explanation 'why' (not a purpose). Ci = 'to/for us' (object/clitic). Trema is present 'trembles' or 'shudders'. E di che congaudete: di che = 'about/over what'; congaudete (congaudere) means 'to be glad about' or 'to take pleasure in' — 'and why it trembles for us and over what you are rejoicing.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g93: Ora = 'now' (adverb). Chi is the interrogative pronoun 'who'. Fosti is the past form (passato remoto) of essere used here to ask 'who were you' (archaic/poetic second-person past or stylistic past addressing the spirit's former identity).
segments.purgatorio-21-g94: Piacciati is an imperative/deferential verb from piacere used reflexively in the sense 'be pleased/deign' (literary formula). Ch' = che 'that'. Io sappia uses the present subjunctive sappia after a wish/request: 'that I may know' (piacciati ch'io sappia = 'please, let me know').
segments.purgatorio-21-g95: E perché = 'and why'. Tanti secoli = 'so many centuries'. Giaciuto is the past participle of giacere 'to lie'; here it participates in a verbal periphrasis with the following se' (an archaic form of sei / 'you are' or a clitic), yielding the meaning 'you have lain' or 'you have been lying'. Qui = here; se’ = archaic clitic for 'you are / you have' (inverting word order is a poetic feature).
segments.purgatorio-21-g96: Ne le parole tue = 'in/from your words' (ne + le parole tue). Mi = 'to me'. Cappia is an older verb (cappiare) meaning literally 'to hood/cover' but here used idiomatically as 'let me be encompassed/let me take in it' — i.e. 'let me learn/receive it from your words' (a somewhat figurative use).
segments.purgatorio-21-g97: Nel is the contraction of in + il 'in the'. Tempo 'time' and che introduces the temporal relative clause 'when'. L' buon Tito = 'the good Titus' (article + adjective before the proper name, a common poetic order).
segments.purgatorio-21-g98: Con = 'with'. L' aiuto = 'the help'. Del = di + il 'of the'. Sommo = 'highest/supreme' (superlative adjective). Rege is an archaic form of reg(e) 'king/ruler'. The whole: 'with the help of the supreme ruler.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g99: Vendicò is passato remoto 'he avenged'. Le fóra (fóra = openings/holes/breaches) is the direct object. Ond' (onde) = 'whence/from which'. Uscì 'came forth/issued' (passato remoto). Sangue = 'blood'. Per Giuda venduto: per = 'by/through' and Giuda venduto = 'Judas the betrayed/traitor' (venduto used as 'betrayer' here).
segments.purgatorio-21-g100: Col = con + il 'with the'. Nome = 'name' or 'reputation'. Che introduces a relative clause: più dura e più onora = 'which lasts more/longer and honors more/most'. Era io di là = 'I was over there' (era = past 'was', di là = 'over there' / 'on that side'). The clause means 'I was over there by the name/reputation that endures and honors most.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g101: Rispuose is passato remoto (third person singular) of rispondere: 'he answered'. Quello spirto = 'that spirit' (quello = that one, spirto = spirit/soul).
segments.purgatorio-21-g102: Famoso 'famous/renowned'; assai is an adverb 'very/much' (here 'very famous'). This is the spirit's self-description.
segments.purgatorio-21-g103: Ma = 'but'. Non = negation. Con fede ancora = 'with faith yet' — ancora here = 'yet/still' in the sense 'not yet': 'but not yet with faith.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g145: Ond' is an elided form (ond' = onde/onde) used here as a discourse connector meaning 'then' or 'thereupon'; io is the emphatic subject pronoun 'I' and stands alone because Dante is introducing direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-21-g146: Forse che is a conjectural phrase meaning 'perhaps that' (introducing a supposition); tu ti maravigli is a reflexive verb construction ('maravigliarsi') with tu (subject) and enclitic ti (reflexive object), maravigli here is a present‑tense form meaning 'you are astonished' (Dante's older morphology but functioning as 2nd‑person present).
segments.purgatorio-21-g147: Antico spirto is a vocative phrase addressing the addressee: antico = 'ancient' (adjective) and spirto is the archaic spelling of spirito ('spirit'/'soul'), used here as a direct address to the deceased soul.
segments.purgatorio-21-g148: del = di + il, marking the object of maravigliarsi ('astonished at'); rider here is a substantive 'laughter' (from the verb ridere); ch' (che) is the relative conjunction 'that' introducing the clause; io fei uses fei, the archaic passato remoto of fare ('I made/did'), so ch'io fei = 'that I made'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g149: ma = 'but'; più d'ammirazion = 'more (of) admiration/amazement' (d' = di); vo' che is an archaic desiderative construction (vo' = voglio), meaning 'I want that...'; ti pigli is subjunctive/present form of prendere used impersonally with ammiração as subject: 'that it seize you' i.e. 'that you be seized by greater amazement.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g150: Questi = 'this one' (demonstrative); che introduces the relative clause 'who guides'; guida is 3rd‑person singular present of guidare ('guides'); in alto is an adverbial phrase 'upward/on high'; li occhi miei = 'my eyes' with the archaic plural article li and the possessive miei placed after the noun (typical poetic order).
segments.purgatorio-21-g151: è = 'is'; quel Virgilio = 'that Virgil' (identifying the person); dal qual = da + il + qual = 'from whom' (relative); tu togliesti is passato remoto 2nd‑person 'you took away' (togliere); forte here is an adverbial/qualifying word (Dante's usage is flexible: note it may mean 'strongly' or function as an intensifier); a cantar is the infinitive 'to sing' with preposition a; de li uomini e d'i dèi = 'of/about men and the gods' (de/d' = di).
segments.purgatorio-21-g152: Se = 'if' (introduces the conditional); cagion altra = 'another cause' (Dante's word order places the adjective after the noun); al mio rider = 'for my laughter' (al = a + il, mio = 'my', rider = 'laughter'); credesti is passato remoto 2nd‑person 'you believed/you thought'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g153: lasciala = lascia + la (imperative 'leave it' or 'let it be'), the enclitic -la refers back to cagion (feminine); per here functions as 'as' or 'considered as'; non vera = 'not true' with vera agreeing in gender with the feminine antecedent.
segments.purgatorio-21-g154: ed = 'and'; esser credi is an instructional sequence where esser (infinitive 'to be') is the complement of credi (imperative/indicative 'believe' used here as a hortative: 'believe [them] to be'); quelle parole = 'those words' (quelle agrees with parole); che di lui dicesti = relative clause 'which you said of him', with dicesti = passato remoto 'you said'.
segments.purgatorio-21-g155: Già = 'already'; s' (si) is the reflexive/clitic particle attached to the verb; inchinava is the imperfect 'was bowing' (3rd‑person); ad abbracciar = 'to embrace' (ad + infinitive); li piedi = 'the feet' (li = definite article, piedi = plural noun); al mio dottor = a + il = 'to my teacher' with mio 'my' and dottor as the noun.
segments.purgatorio-21-g156: ma = 'but'; el is the archaic subject pronoun 'he' (egli); li here functions as an object/clitic meaning 'to him' (Danteian clitic order: el li disse = 'he said to him'); disse is passato remoto 'he said.'
segments.purgatorio-21-g157: Frate is a vocative 'Brother' (addressing the person), a direct form of address used by the speaker in the reported speech.
segments.purgatorio-21-g158: non far = imperative negative 'do not do' (non = not; far = archaic infinitive/imperative form of fare = 'do').
segments.purgatorio-21-g159: ché = 'for/because' introducing the reason; tu se' (se' = sei in Dantean spelling) = 'you are' (present of essere); ombra = 'a shade/shadow' (here 'a spirit/ghost'); e ombra vedi = 'and you see a shade' (vedi = present 'you see' or 'you perceive'), repeating ombra for emphasis.

sentences.purgatorio-21-s13: He began: "Is there anything that, without order, feels the mountain's devotion, or that lies outside customary practice?"
sentences.purgatorio-21-s14: Here it is free from every alteration: what Heaven, from itself in itself, receives can be the cause of our being, and not anything else.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s15: Therefore no rain, no hail, no snow, no dew, no hoarfrost falls any higher than the short little stair of three steps; thick clouds neither appear dense nor sparse, nor lightning, nor the daughter of Thaumas (Iris), who over there often changes her quarters; dry vapor does not rise any farther forward than the summit of the three steps I spoke of, where the vicar of Peter has his feet.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s16: It may tremble farther down a little or a lot; but because of wind that hides itself in the earth — I do not know how — up here it never trembled.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s37: And he, rising, said: "Now you can grasp the measure of the love that warms me toward you, when I forget our vanity, treating the shades as if they were solid matter."
sentences.purgatorio-21-s29: Virgil turned these words to me with a look which, while keeping silent, said 'Be silent'; but virtue cannot do everything it wants; for laughter and tears are such followers of the passion by which each person is marked, that they follow the will less in the truest (people).
sentences.purgatorio-21-s30: I too smiled like a man who winks; whereupon the shade fell silent, and he looked at me in the eyes where his countenance is most fixed; and he said, "If so much toil amounts to good, why does your very face show me a flash of laughter?"
sentences.purgatorio-21-s32: Now I am seized on one side and the other: one urges me to be silent, the other implores that I speak; whereupon I sigh, and I am heard by my master, and he says to me, "Do not be afraid to speak; but speak and tell him what he asks with such care."
sentences.purgatorio-21-s6: , he said, and we went on quickly: "If you are shades whom God on high does not deign, who has escorted you so far up his ladder?"
sentences.purgatorio-21-s8: And my teacher: "If you look at the signs that this one bears and that the angel outlines, you will clearly see that it is fitting that he should reign with the good."
sentences.purgatorio-21-s17: They tremble here whenever any soul, made clean, feels itself, so that she rises or stirs to climb up; and such a cry follows.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s18: Only the will puts purity to the test; for, being wholly free to change its dwelling, it seizes the soul and helps her in willing.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s19: At first the will is good, but Divine Justice does not abandon the inclination which, against her will — as it was in sinning — places her in torment.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s20: And I, who have lain in this suffering five hundred years and more, even now felt a free will toward a better threshold: therefore you heard the tremor and the pious spirits through the mountain render praise to that Lord, who straightaway sends them up.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s1: The natural thirst that is never satisfied except by the water for which the little Samaritan woman asked grace tormented me; and haste pricked me on account of the awkward path behind my guide, and I felt sympathy for the just retribution.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s2: And behold, just as Luke records that Christ appeared to the two who were on the road — already risen out of the sepulchral pit — a shade appeared to us, and was coming behind us, looking from the foot at the crowd that lies there; nor did we take notice of her, for she spoke first, saying: 'O my brothers, may God grant you peace.'
sentences.purgatorio-21-s3: We immediately turned ourselves, and Virgil made the sign appropriate to that.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s4: Then he began: 'May the true court that consigns me to eternal exile place you in peace in the blessed council.'
sentences.purgatorio-21-s25: So sweet was my vocal spirit, that Rome drew me to herself — Tolosano — where I deserved to adorn my temples with myrtle.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s26: The people over there still call me Statius: I sang of Thebes, and then of great Achilles; but I fell by the way with the second burden.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s27: From my ardour the sparks were seed, which warmed me from the divine flame whence more than a thousand have been kindled; I mean the Aeneid, which was my mother and my nurse while I was composing: without it I would not have restrained the burden of drama.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s28: And because he lived there at the time when Virgil lived, I would assent one sun more than I ought to regarding my coming out of banishment.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s9: But because she, who spins day and night, had not yet drawn out for them the distaff that Clotho sets upon each and completes,
sentences.purgatorio-21-s10: Whence I was drawn out from the wide throat of Hell to show them, and to show them further, how far my teaching can lead (them).
sentences.purgatorio-21-s11: But tell me, if you know, why such crashes struck the mountain just now, and why everything at once seemed to cry out even down to its soft feet."
sentences.purgatorio-21-s12: So he gave me (that answer), asking — through the eye of my desire — that even with hope my thirst became less famished.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s21: Thus he said of it; and because he enjoys drinking so much — so great is his thirst — I cannot say how brave he made me.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s22: And the wise duke: 'By now I see the snare that entangles you here and how it thrashes about,'
sentences.purgatorio-21-s23: Now who were you, please, that I may know, and why you have lain here so many centuries — let me learn it from your words.'
sentences.purgatorio-21-s24: 'At the time when the good Titus, with the help of the supreme ruler, avenged the breaches whence came the blood of Judas the betrayer, I was over there by the name that endures longest and brings the most honor,' replied that spirit, 'very famous indeed, but not yet with faith.'
sentences.purgatorio-21-s33: Then I said: "Perhaps you are astonished, ancient spirit, at the laugh I made; but I would rather that greater amazement seize you."
sentences.purgatorio-21-s34: This one who guides my eyes upward is that Virgil, from whom you took away (strongly) the power to sing of men and of the gods.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s35: If you believed another cause for my laughter, leave it as not true, and believe those words which you said about him.
sentences.purgatorio-21-s36: Already he was bowing to embrace my teacher's feet, but he said to him: "Brother, do not do it, for you are a shade and you see a shade."
